question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: ### Short answer Most likely this is a perimeter (WAF / reverse‑proxy) problem: a recent security/patch activity caused the WAF to reload a default inspection/profile and the Portal‑App‑Template (with the custom header‑rewrite that forwards X‑Forwarded‑Proto) was not active — as a result the backend did not see X‑Forwarded‑Proto and returned 502s for external FQDN requests. This is a 2nd‑level (Network & Security / WAF) support issue and should be escalated accordingly.
context: SOURCE 15: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF40: Customer Portal – Login via external URL not possible Ticket number: TI-1594408 Company: Customer Company 40 AG Contact person: Contact Person 19 Owner: Employee 29 Origin: Phone Type: Incident Created on: 03.06.25 07:52 Closure Closure date: 18.06.25 15:33 Closure text: Analysis completed. Configuration adjusted according to architectural specifications. Function restored. Activities 03.06.25 07:45 Phone report. Issue: External users can no longer log in to the customer portal. URL: https://portal.kf40.ch Browser error message: 502 – Bad Gateway Internally (via internal network) login works. Priority: High (customers affected) 03.06.25 07:52 Ticket created Assigned to: Employee 29 Status: In Progress Notes 03.06.25 08:30 Helpdesk checks: • External DNS resolution correct • Certificate valid • HTTPS reachable • Internal backend (app server) reachable Test: External → 502 Internal → works Suspicion: Reverse Proxy / WAF 03.06.25 09:10 Customer adds: A new certificate was installed over the weekend. Additionally, there was a firewall update (security patch). 03.06.25 10:25 Helpdesk checks firewall: • Rule for port 443 present • NAT correct • No blocking visible in logs Load balancer status: Backend server marked as “Healthy”. 03.06.25 11:40 Test with direct IP to Reverse Proxy → works. Test via FQDN → 502 Notable: Header “X-Forwarded-Proto” missing in external request. 04.06.25 09:05 Customer asks whether certificate may be incorrectly bound. Certificate validation: • CN correct • SAN correct • Chain complete No obvious errors. 05.06.25 08:50 Ticket forwarded to Network / Security Team. Assigned group changed to: BU Network & Security 06.06.25 09:30 Taken over by System Engineer 21 WAF analysis: • Policy active • TLS inspection active • HTTP/2 enabled Notable: After patch, default profile was reloaded. 06.06.25 11:15 Test: TLS inspection temporarily disabled → Login works. Inspection re-enabled → 502 again. 09.06.25 10:05 Further analysis: App server expects header: X-Forwarded-Proto: https When TLS inspection is active, header is not forwarded. 09.06.25 14:40 Comparison with architecture documentation performed. Reference in internal document: “Blueprint: Secure Web Publishing – KF40 – Version 3.2” Section 4.3 describes special header forwarding in case of TLS offloading. 10.06.25 08:25 Blueprint document loaded from DMS. Relevant notes: • TLS termination must occur on WAF • Backend expects HTTPS signaling • Custom header rewrite rule required • Inspection policy must not remove HTTP headers • Specific exception defined for portal application 10.06.25 11:45 Configuration comparison with blueprint shows: • Custom header rewrite rule missing • HTTP profile does not match documented version • Inspection template “Default-Strict” active instead of “Portal-App-Template” 12.06.25 Internal coordination: Firewall patch overwrote template. Custom policy was not reassigned. 16.06.25 Configuration reviewed and adjusted according to blueprint. No technical details documented in ticket (security policy). 18.06.25 14:55 External access test successful. Multiple login attempts tested. Monitoring stable for 24 hours. Ticket closure 18.06.25 15:33 Dear Contact Person 19 The root cause was a deviation in the Reverse Proxy configuration from the documented architectural specification following a security patch. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal blueprint documentation. The customer portal is now fully accessible again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 13: # KF40 Portal Publication (portal.kf40.ch) — WAF/Reverse-Proxy and Firewall Operational Community This community centers on the perimeter publishing stack (WAF / Reverse Proxy) that publishes the Customer Company 40 portal (portal.kf40.ch) and the network/firewall estate that supports user connectivity (including Teams media). A high‑priority support ticket (TI-1594408) documented an external 502 Bad Gateway outage for portal.kf40.ch while internal access remained functional. Troubleshooting showed the backend application servers and load balancer were healthy, TLS certificates were validated and not the root cause, and the outage correlated to a weekend security patch/change that reloaded a Default-Strict inspection/profile and removed a custom header rewrite rule needed to present X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend. Restoring the Portal-App-Template / custom header rewrite configuration per the Secure Web Publishing blueprint resolved external access. Separately, a Zurich site firewall hardening change restricted outbound UDP to ports 3478–3481 only, blocking the dynamic RTP port range and causing one‑way/no audio in Teams; a temporary port opening restored media flows and confirmed the root cause. The incident and artifacts highlight technical, process, and governance gaps around post‑patch template/profile validation, change control for perimeter devices, and runbooks for header/HTTP profile preservation during TLS inspection/termination. ## Root cause of portal 502: WAF TLS inspection plus missing/custom header rewrite rule (X-Forwarded-Proto) prevented the backend from seeing HTTPS. External requests to portal.kf40.ch returned 502 Bad Gateway while internal access remained functional; investigation showed the reverse proxy/WAF performs TLS termination/inspection and is expected to forward X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend, but that header was not present when inspection was active, causing the backend to misinterpret the request and return errors. This causal chain is documented in the incident artifacts linking the REVERSE PROXY / WAF to the APPLICATION SERVER and noting the missing CUSTOM HEADER REWRITE RULE and absent X-FORWARDED-PROTO header as drivers for the 502s [Data: Entities (302,312,319); Relationships (492,499,496)]. Restoring the header rewrite behavior per the Portal-App-Template/blueprint returned external functionality, confirming the header rewrite absence (not the backend or certificate validity) was the functional trigger for failures [Data: Entities (311,300); Relationships (568,499)]. ## Security patch/template reload replaced Portal-App-Template with Default-Strict, causing template/profile drift and header removal. A weekend security patch caused the reverse proxy/WAF to reload a default inspection/profile (Default-Strict) which replaced the custom PORTAL-APP-TEMPLATE and its documented HTTP/profile/header‑rewrite rules; the activated Default-Strict profile altered header-handling and removed or failed to preserve X-Forwarded headers, reproducing the 502 when TLS inspection was enabled [Data: Entities (310,311); Relationships (497,568,548)]. This shows configuration/template drift introduced by patching—where a device reset to a default profile overwrote application‑specific templates—rather than an application or certificate error, creating a clear process risk for perimeter devices that host per-application templates [Data: Relationships (497,562)]. ## Backend and load‑balancing layer were healthy—issue isolated to publishing/inspection layer. Load balancer health checks and internal tests marked the backend application server as healthy and reachable, indicating the backend process was not the primary failure point; monitoring and health checks were used to validate stability after the fix before ticket closure [Data: Entities (305,318,308); Relationships (504,505,513)]. These diagnostics materially constrained the fault domain to the perimeter (WAF/reverse proxy) and saved time by directing remediation to template/profile/header handling rather than application restarts alone [Data: Relationships (492,563)]. ## TLS certificate change was investigated and ruled out as the root cause. The customer reported a recently installed TLS certificate and helpdesk validated the certificate CN, SAN and chain; while the certificate change was temporally proximate to the outage, diagnostics showed certificate validity/chain issues were not the cause of the 502s—the publishing layer header behavior was [Data: Entities (306); Relationships (486)]. The certificate service and blueprint decision tree were consulted as part of the normal checklist, which helped rule TLS problems out and focus on WAF behavior [Data: Relationships (552,508)]. ## Teams media outage at Zurich caused by outbound firewall restriction blocking dynamic RTP ports. A separate but contemporaneous operational change at the Zurich site implemented a security-hardening outbound rule that permitted only UDP 3478–3481, blocking the dynamic RTP port range required for Teams media when Media Bypass is used. This change produced no audio for external calls; temporarily opening the wider UDP/RTP range restored audio immediately, confirming the firewall rule as the root cause [Data: Entities (303,442,443); Relationships (739,741,742,733)]. Firewall logs and change records tied the timing of the outbound restriction to the onset of audio issues, showing this was a local change management failure with direct impact to voice/media availability [Data: Relationships (738,739)]. ## Blueprint and operational docs exist but were not enforced by the post‑patch validation process. An authoritative Secure Web Publishing blueprint and versioned guidance (BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2 / WEB PUBLISHING BLUEPRINT) defines required behaviors: TLS termination at the WAF, required Portal-App-Template, and documented header rewrite rules and validation after patches. The team retrieved the blueprint from DMS to align remediation, indicating awareness of the standard; however, the blueprint's requirement to validate WAF profiles and preserve custom rewrite rules was not enforced automatically during the patch that reset profiles, producing configuration drift [Data: Entities (307,339,356); Relationships (508,512,562)]. This gap between blueprint and operational enforcement—where a profile reload caused a prohibited Default-Strict to be applied despite the blueprint forbidding it—exposes the environment to repeat regressions unless validation and change controls are hardened [Data: Relationships (548,497)]. ## Operational ownership, escalation path, and remediation were used correctly but could be formalized into runbooks and automated checks. The high-priority ticket TI-1594408 was owned initially by Employee 29 (helpdesk) who performed initial checks and escalated to BU Network & Security; BU Network & Security then took ownership and applied WAF/reverse proxy configuration changes to restore service, with monitoring confirming 24 hours stability before closure—showing correct functional escalation and remediation occurred [Data: Entities (295,298,299); Relationships (479,487,476)]. The artifacts show manual steps and handoffs (tests, template restores, monitoring) that resolved the incident, but the manual nature of these actions recommends formalizing a post‑patch WAF validation runbook, automated configuration drift detection, and explicit pre/post patch checklists to reduce mean‑time‑to‑repair and recurrence risk [Data: Relationships (479,513)].

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 17: ﻿ general info & description This document describes the blueprint for the secure publication of web-based applications of Customer Company 40 over the Internet. The architecture is based on a Reverse Proxy and Web Application Firewall (WAF) concept with TLS termination at the perimeter level. The objective is to protect internal application servers from direct Internet exposure while ensuring secure and controlled communication with external users. The Web Publishing architecture is used for the following applications: • Customer portal (portal.kf40.ch) • Partner access • Internal web services with external interface Central principles: • No direct publication of internal servers • TLS termination exclusively on the WAF • Backend servers are located in a separate server VLAN • HTTPS semantics must be preserved for the backend • Standardized templates for all portal applications Best practices: • Use of dedicated WAF templates per application • Documented header rewrite rules • No use of generic “Default-Strict” profiles for productive portals • Validation against this blueprint after every security patch MUST BE OBSERVED: All changes to WAF profiles, TLS policies, or Reverse Proxy rules must be validated against this document. design The architecture consists of three logical zones: Internet → Perimeter Zone (WAF / Reverse Proxy) → Internal Application Zone Components 1. Web Application Firewall (WAF) o TLS termination o Inspection policy o Header rewrite o Load balancing 2. Reverse Proxy Layer o Forwarding to application servers o Host header handling o Session persistence 3. Backend Application Server o Expects HTTPS semantics o No direct Internet access o Internal authentication TLS Offloading TLS is terminated on the WAF. Communication between the WAF and backend is performed over HTTP by default. In order for the backend to correctly interpret the original HTTPS request, the following headers must be set: • X-Forwarded-Proto: https • X-Forwarded-Host These headers must not be removed or overwritten by inspection or security profiles. WAF Template For portal applications, the template: Portal-App-Template must be used. This template contains: • Header rewrite rules • HTTPS semantics preservation • HTTP/2 compatibility configuration • Session handling • Adjustments for authentication flows The use of other templates (e.g., Default-Strict) is not permitted for productive portal services. After Security Patches After firmware or security updates, it is mandatory to verify: • Whether the correct template is still active • Whether custom rewrite rules remain in place • Whether inspection policies have not been reset • Whether TLS profiles remain unchanged decision tree & use cases 1. Question: Is the application reachable internally but not externally? Yes → Proceed to question 2. No → The issue is likely not within the Web Publishing layer. 2. Question: Is a 502 or 503 error displayed during external access? Yes → Check WAF template and header rewrite rules. No → Proceed to question 3. 3. Question: Is there a redirect loop between HTTP and HTTPS? Yes → Check X-Forwarded-Proto header. No → Proceed to question 4. 4. Question: Was a certificate recently renewed or a security patch applied? Yes → Validate configuration according to section “After Security Patches”. No → Proceed to question 5. 5. Question: Is TLS inspection enabled? Yes → Verify that the Portal-App-Template is used. No → Check backend health status.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 17: ﻿ general info & description This document describes the blueprint for the secure publication of web-based applications of Customer Company 40 over the Internet. The architecture is based on a Reverse Proxy and Web Application Firewall (WAF) concept with TLS termination at the perimeter level. The objective is to protect internal application servers from direct Internet exposure while ensuring secure and controlled communication with external users. The Web Publishing architecture is used for the following applications: • Customer portal (portal.kf40.ch) • Partner access • Internal web services with external interface Central principles: • No direct publication of internal servers • TLS termination exclusively on the WAF • Backend servers are located in a separate server VLAN • HTTPS semantics must be preserved for the backend • Standardized templates for all portal applications Best practices: • Use of dedicated WAF templates per application • Documented header rewrite rules • No use of generic “Default-Strict” profiles for productive portals • Validation against this blueprint after every security patch MUST BE OBSERVED: All changes to WAF profiles, TLS policies, or Reverse Proxy rules must be validated against this document. design The architecture consists of three logical zones: Internet → Perimeter Zone (WAF / Reverse Proxy) → Internal Application Zone Components 1. Web Application Firewall (WAF) o TLS termination o Inspection policy o Header rewrite o Load balancing 2. Reverse Proxy Layer o Forwarding to application servers o Host header handling o Session persistence 3. Backend Application Server o Expects HTTPS semantics o No direct Internet access o Internal authentication TLS Offloading TLS is terminated on the WAF. Communication between the WAF and backend is performed over HTTP by default. In order for the backend to correctly interpret the original HTTPS request, the following headers must be set: • X-Forwarded-Proto: https • X-Forwarded-Host These headers must not be removed or overwritten by inspection or security profiles. WAF Template For portal applications, the template: Portal-App-Template must be used. This template contains: • Header rewrite rules • HTTPS semantics preservation • HTTP/2 compatibility configuration • Session handling • Adjustments for authentication flows The use of other templates (e.g., Default-Strict) is not permitted for productive portal services. After Security Patches After firmware or security updates, it is mandatory to verify: • Whether the correct template is still active • Whether custom rewrite rules remain in place • Whether inspection policies have not been reset • Whether TLS profiles remain unchanged decision tree & use cases 1. Question: Is the application reachable internally but not externally? Yes → Proceed to question 2. No → The issue is likely not within the Web Publishing layer. 2. Question: Is a 502 or 503 error displayed during external access? Yes → Check WAF template and header rewrite rules. No → Proceed to question 3. 3. Question: Is there a redirect loop between HTTP and HTTPS? Yes → Check X-Forwarded-Proto header. No → Proceed to question 4. 4. Question: Was a certificate recently renewed or a security patch applied? Yes → Validate configuration according to section “After Security Patches”. No → Proceed to question 5. 5. Question: Is TLS inspection enabled? Yes → Verify that the Portal-App-Template is used. No → Check backend health status.

context: REPORT 13: # KF40 Portal Publication (portal.kf40.ch) — WAF/Reverse-Proxy and Firewall Operational Community This community centers on the perimeter publishing stack (WAF / Reverse Proxy) that publishes the Customer Company 40 portal (portal.kf40.ch) and the network/firewall estate that supports user connectivity (including Teams media). A high‑priority support ticket (TI-1594408) documented an external 502 Bad Gateway outage for portal.kf40.ch while internal access remained functional. Troubleshooting showed the backend application servers and load balancer were healthy, TLS certificates were validated and not the root cause, and the outage correlated to a weekend security patch/change that reloaded a Default-Strict inspection/profile and removed a custom header rewrite rule needed to present X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend. Restoring the Portal-App-Template / custom header rewrite configuration per the Secure Web Publishing blueprint resolved external access. Separately, a Zurich site firewall hardening change restricted outbound UDP to ports 3478–3481 only, blocking the dynamic RTP port range and causing one‑way/no audio in Teams; a temporary port opening restored media flows and confirmed the root cause. The incident and artifacts highlight technical, process, and governance gaps around post‑patch template/profile validation, change control for perimeter devices, and runbooks for header/HTTP profile preservation during TLS inspection/termination. ## Root cause of portal 502: WAF TLS inspection plus missing/custom header rewrite rule (X-Forwarded-Proto) prevented the backend from seeing HTTPS. External requests to portal.kf40.ch returned 502 Bad Gateway while internal access remained functional; investigation showed the reverse proxy/WAF performs TLS termination/inspection and is expected to forward X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend, but that header was not present when inspection was active, causing the backend to misinterpret the request and return errors. This causal chain is documented in the incident artifacts linking the REVERSE PROXY / WAF to the APPLICATION SERVER and noting the missing CUSTOM HEADER REWRITE RULE and absent X-FORWARDED-PROTO header as drivers for the 502s [Data: Entities (302,312,319); Relationships (492,499,496)]. Restoring the header rewrite behavior per the Portal-App-Template/blueprint returned external functionality, confirming the header rewrite absence (not the backend or certificate validity) was the functional trigger for failures [Data: Entities (311,300); Relationships (568,499)]. ## Security patch/template reload replaced Portal-App-Template with Default-Strict, causing template/profile drift and header removal. A weekend security patch caused the reverse proxy/WAF to reload a default inspection/profile (Default-Strict) which replaced the custom PORTAL-APP-TEMPLATE and its documented HTTP/profile/header‑rewrite rules; the activated Default-Strict profile altered header-handling and removed or failed to preserve X-Forwarded headers, reproducing the 502 when TLS inspection was enabled [Data: Entities (310,311); Relationships (497,568,548)]. This shows configuration/template drift introduced by patching—where a device reset to a default profile overwrote application‑specific templates—rather than an application or certificate error, creating a clear process risk for perimeter devices that host per-application templates [Data: Relationships (497,562)]. ## Backend and load‑balancing layer were healthy—issue isolated to publishing/inspection layer. Load balancer health checks and internal tests marked the backend application server as healthy and reachable, indicating the backend process was not the primary failure point; monitoring and health checks were used to validate stability after the fix before ticket closure [Data: Entities (305,318,308); Relationships (504,505,513)]. These diagnostics materially constrained the fault domain to the perimeter (WAF/reverse proxy) and saved time by directing remediation to template/profile/header handling rather than application restarts alone [Data: Relationships (492,563)]. ## TLS certificate change was investigated and ruled out as the root cause. The customer reported a recently installed TLS certificate and helpdesk validated the certificate CN, SAN and chain; while the certificate change was temporally proximate to the outage, diagnostics showed certificate validity/chain issues were not the cause of the 502s—the publishing layer header behavior was [Data: Entities (306); Relationships (486)]. The certificate service and blueprint decision tree were consulted as part of the normal checklist, which helped rule TLS problems out and focus on WAF behavior [Data: Relationships (552,508)]. ## Teams media outage at Zurich caused by outbound firewall restriction blocking dynamic RTP ports. A separate but contemporaneous operational change at the Zurich site implemented a security-hardening outbound rule that permitted only UDP 3478–3481, blocking the dynamic RTP port range required for Teams media when Media Bypass is used. This change produced no audio for external calls; temporarily opening the wider UDP/RTP range restored audio immediately, confirming the firewall rule as the root cause [Data: Entities (303,442,443); Relationships (739,741,742,733)]. Firewall logs and change records tied the timing of the outbound restriction to the onset of audio issues, showing this was a local change management failure with direct impact to voice/media availability [Data: Relationships (738,739)]. ## Blueprint and operational docs exist but were not enforced by the post‑patch validation process. An authoritative Secure Web Publishing blueprint and versioned guidance (BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2 / WEB PUBLISHING BLUEPRINT) defines required behaviors: TLS termination at the WAF, required Portal-App-Template, and documented header rewrite rules and validation after patches. The team retrieved the blueprint from DMS to align remediation, indicating awareness of the standard; however, the blueprint's requirement to validate WAF profiles and preserve custom rewrite rules was not enforced automatically during the patch that reset profiles, producing configuration drift [Data: Entities (307,339,356); Relationships (508,512,562)]. This gap between blueprint and operational enforcement—where a profile reload caused a prohibited Default-Strict to be applied despite the blueprint forbidding it—exposes the environment to repeat regressions unless validation and change controls are hardened [Data: Relationships (548,497)]. ## Operational ownership, escalation path, and remediation were used correctly but could be formalized into runbooks and automated checks. The high-priority ticket TI-1594408 was owned initially by Employee 29 (helpdesk) who performed initial checks and escalated to BU Network & Security; BU Network & Security then took ownership and applied WAF/reverse proxy configuration changes to restore service, with monitoring confirming 24 hours stability before closure—showing correct functional escalation and remediation occurred [Data: Entities (295,298,299); Relationships (479,487,476)]. The artifacts show manual steps and handoffs (tests, template restores, monitoring) that resolved the incident, but the manual nature of these actions recommends formalizing a post‑patch WAF validation runbook, automated configuration drift detection, and explicit pre/post patch checklists to reduce mean‑time‑to‑repair and recurrence risk [Data: Relationships (479,513)]. REPORT 159: # Reverse Proxy/WAF → Application Server: X-Forwarded-Proto Header Failure The community centers on the external Reverse Proxy / WAF which fronts the internal APPLICATION SERVER hosting the TRAIN CE portal. TLS inspection/termination on the WAF removed or failed to forward the X-Forwarded-Proto header expected by the backend, producing 502 errors visible externally. A security patch or template reload replaced the intended Portal-App-Template with a different template (Default-Strict) and the custom header rewrite rule required to restore X-Forwarded-Proto was missing; temporarily disabling TLS inspection allowed successful logins until the WAF configuration was corrected. The core interactions, responsibilities, and failure mode are documented in the relationships between the WAF, TLS inspection, the rewrite rule, the X-Forwarded-Proto header, and the APPLICATION SERVER. [Data: Entities (302, 236, 312, 313, 319), Relationships (492, 499, 500, 496, 506)] ## Reverse Proxy / WAF is the central choke point for externally-visible availability. All externally-observed failures trace to the WAF/reverse proxy which performs TLS termination/inspection and publishes the portal; it is the upstream component responsible for correct header forwarding to the backend. The WAF's TLS inspection and template/configuration behavior directly affected requests sent to the APPLICATION SERVER and produced the observed 502s. [Data: Entities (302); Relationships (492, 500)] ## Missing custom header rewrite rule is the immediate root cause of backend signaling mismatch. The application expects the upstream to provide X-Forwarded-Proto: https; when the custom rewrite rule on the WAF was absent, backends received requests without the expected HTTPS signaling and returned errors. Restoring or recreating the rewrite rule on the reverse proxy is the direct fix to restore correct backend behavior. [Data: Entities (312, 319); Relationships (499, 506)] ## TLS inspection behavior removed or failed to forward X-Forwarded-Proto, reproducing 502s. Ticket tests showed that disabling TLS inspection allowed external logins to succeed, while re-enabling inspection reproduced 502 errors—indicating the inspection/termination process removed or failed to forward the header unless explicitly handled. This identifies TLS inspection as the mechanism that exposed the missing-rewrite misconfiguration. [Data: Entities (313, 302); Relationships (500, 496)] ## APPLICATION SERVER relies on upstream header (not on local TLS) to determine HTTPS context. The backend process component hosting TRAIN CE expects the upstream proxy/load balancer to provide X-Forwarded-Proto: https and responded successfully in internal (non-proxied) tests; load balancer health checks reported the backend healthy. Given these internal successes, externally visible failures are symptomatic of the proxy's header behavior rather than an application process failure. [Data: Entities (236, 319); Relationships (492, 506)] ## Configuration/template drift after a security patch caused the missing rule and deviation from blueprint. The WAF had its inspection template changed after a security patch (Default-Strict loaded instead of Portal-App-Template) and the custom header rewrite rule was missing following that reload—this configuration drift directly contributed to service disruption. The environment must treat template reloads and security updates as change events that can remove required customizations. [Data: Entities (302); Relationships (499)] ## Clear division of responsibility: WAF team must own header rewrite; application team must validate header expectations. Record guidance indicates the WAF should host the custom header rewrite and the application depends on the header being present; operational ownership should reflect that: WAF/reverse-proxy owners are responsible for TLS offload behavior and rewrite rules, while APPLICATION SERVER owners are responsible for documenting upstream expectations and verifying behavior after WAF/template changes. Escalations during incidents should route between these owners. [Data: Entities (312, 236, 302); Relationships (499, 506)] ## Practical immediate mitigations and runbook steps (short-term, actionable). Short-term restoration steps validated by the ticketed tests include: temporarily disabling TLS inspection to restore user access (workaround), reloading or restoring the Portal-App-Template (or re-applying the custom rewrite rule), and verifying that X-Forwarded-Proto is present on backend requests. Each step should be executed in coordination between WAF operators and application owners with a rollback plan for security controls. These mitigations are supported by observed behavior where disabling inspection allowed logins and restoring rewrite logic corrected the 502s. [Data: Entities (313, 312, 302); Relationships (500, 499, 492)] ## Recommended medium/long-term controls: blueprint enforcement, change-control, monitoring and automated validation. To reduce recurrence, enforce the 'Secure Web Publishing' blueprint for TLS offload scenarios, add pre- and post-change validation checks when templates or patches are applied, implement automated smoke tests that validate presence of X-Forwarded-Proto and that exercise portal login flows, and add alerting on spikes in 502s tied to WAF changes. These governance controls address the exact failure mode documented where template reloads removed a required header rewrite and caused customer-impacting errors. [Data: Entities (302); Relationships (492, 499)]

context: SOURCE 17: ﻿ general info & description This document describes the blueprint for the secure publication of web-based applications of Customer Company 40 over the Internet. The architecture is based on a Reverse Proxy and Web Application Firewall (WAF) concept with TLS termination at the perimeter level. The objective is to protect internal application servers from direct Internet exposure while ensuring secure and controlled communication with external users. The Web Publishing architecture is used for the following applications: • Customer portal (portal.kf40.ch) • Partner access • Internal web services with external interface Central principles: • No direct publication of internal servers • TLS termination exclusively on the WAF • Backend servers are located in a separate server VLAN • HTTPS semantics must be preserved for the backend • Standardized templates for all portal applications Best practices: • Use of dedicated WAF templates per application • Documented header rewrite rules • No use of generic “Default-Strict” profiles for productive portals • Validation against this blueprint after every security patch MUST BE OBSERVED: All changes to WAF profiles, TLS policies, or Reverse Proxy rules must be validated against this document. design The architecture consists of three logical zones: Internet → Perimeter Zone (WAF / Reverse Proxy) → Internal Application Zone Components 1. Web Application Firewall (WAF) o TLS termination o Inspection policy o Header rewrite o Load balancing 2. Reverse Proxy Layer o Forwarding to application servers o Host header handling o Session persistence 3. Backend Application Server o Expects HTTPS semantics o No direct Internet access o Internal authentication TLS Offloading TLS is terminated on the WAF. Communication between the WAF and backend is performed over HTTP by default. In order for the backend to correctly interpret the original HTTPS request, the following headers must be set: • X-Forwarded-Proto: https • X-Forwarded-Host These headers must not be removed or overwritten by inspection or security profiles. WAF Template For portal applications, the template: Portal-App-Template must be used. This template contains: • Header rewrite rules • HTTPS semantics preservation • HTTP/2 compatibility configuration • Session handling • Adjustments for authentication flows The use of other templates (e.g., Default-Strict) is not permitted for productive portal services. After Security Patches After firmware or security updates, it is mandatory to verify: • Whether the correct template is still active • Whether custom rewrite rules remain in place • Whether inspection policies have not been reset • Whether TLS profiles remain unchanged decision tree & use cases 1. Question: Is the application reachable internally but not externally? Yes → Proceed to question 2. No → The issue is likely not within the Web Publishing layer. 2. Question: Is a 502 or 503 error displayed during external access? Yes → Check WAF template and header rewrite rules. No → Proceed to question 3. 3. Question: Is there a redirect loop between HTTP and HTTPS? Yes → Check X-Forwarded-Proto header. No → Proceed to question 4. 4. Question: Was a certificate recently renewed or a security patch applied? Yes → Validate configuration according to section “After Security Patches”. No → Proceed to question 5. 5. Question: Is TLS inspection enabled? Yes → Verify that the Portal-App-Template is used. No → Check backend health status. REPORT 13: # KF40 Portal Publication (portal.kf40.ch) — WAF/Reverse-Proxy and Firewall Operational Community This community centers on the perimeter publishing stack (WAF / Reverse Proxy) that publishes the Customer Company 40 portal (portal.kf40.ch) and the network/firewall estate that supports user connectivity (including Teams media). A high‑priority support ticket (TI-1594408) documented an external 502 Bad Gateway outage for portal.kf40.ch while internal access remained functional. Troubleshooting showed the backend application servers and load balancer were healthy, TLS certificates were validated and not the root cause, and the outage correlated to a weekend security patch/change that reloaded a Default-Strict inspection/profile and removed a custom header rewrite rule needed to present X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend. Restoring the Portal-App-Template / custom header rewrite configuration per the Secure Web Publishing blueprint resolved external access. Separately, a Zurich site firewall hardening change restricted outbound UDP to ports 3478–3481 only, blocking the dynamic RTP port range and causing one‑way/no audio in Teams; a temporary port opening restored media flows and confirmed the root cause. The incident and artifacts highlight technical, process, and governance gaps around post‑patch template/profile validation, change control for perimeter devices, and runbooks for header/HTTP profile preservation during TLS inspection/termination. ## Root cause of portal 502: WAF TLS inspection plus missing/custom header rewrite rule (X-Forwarded-Proto) prevented the backend from seeing HTTPS. External requests to portal.kf40.ch returned 502 Bad Gateway while internal access remained functional; investigation showed the reverse proxy/WAF performs TLS termination/inspection and is expected to forward X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend, but that header was not present when inspection was active, causing the backend to misinterpret the request and return errors. This causal chain is documented in the incident artifacts linking the REVERSE PROXY / WAF to the APPLICATION SERVER and noting the missing CUSTOM HEADER REWRITE RULE and absent X-FORWARDED-PROTO header as drivers for the 502s [Data: Entities (302,312,319); Relationships (492,499,496)]. Restoring the header rewrite behavior per the Portal-App-Template/blueprint returned external functionality, confirming the header rewrite absence (not the backend or certificate validity) was the functional trigger for failures [Data: Entities (311,300); Relationships (568,499)]. ## Security patch/template reload replaced Portal-App-Template with Default-Strict, causing template/profile drift and header removal. A weekend security patch caused the reverse proxy/WAF to reload a default inspection/profile (Default-Strict) which replaced the custom PORTAL-APP-TEMPLATE and its documented HTTP/profile/header‑rewrite rules; the activated Default-Strict profile altered header-handling and removed or failed to preserve X-Forwarded headers, reproducing the 502 when TLS inspection was enabled [Data: Entities (310,311); Relationships (497,568,548)]. This shows configuration/template drift introduced by patching—where a device reset to a default profile overwrote application‑specific templates—rather than an application or certificate error, creating a clear process risk for perimeter devices that host per-application templates [Data: Relationships (497,562)]. ## Backend and load‑balancing layer were healthy—issue isolated to publishing/inspection layer. Load balancer health checks and internal tests marked the backend application server as healthy and reachable, indicating the backend process was not the primary failure point; monitoring and health checks were used to validate stability after the fix before ticket closure [Data: Entities (305,318,308); Relationships (504,505,513)]. These diagnostics materially constrained the fault domain to the perimeter (WAF/reverse proxy) and saved time by directing remediation to template/profile/header handling rather than application restarts alone [Data: Relationships (492,563)]. ## TLS certificate change was investigated and ruled out as the root cause. The customer reported a recently installed TLS certificate and helpdesk validated the certificate CN, SAN and chain; while the certificate change was temporally proximate to the outage, diagnostics showed certificate validity/chain issues were not the cause of the 502s—the publishing layer header behavior was [Data: Entities (306); Relationships (486)]. The certificate service and blueprint decision tree were consulted as part of the normal checklist, which helped rule TLS problems out and focus on WAF behavior [Data: Relationships (552,508)]. ## Teams media outage at Zurich caused by outbound firewall restriction blocking dynamic RTP ports. A separate but contemporaneous operational change at the Zurich site implemented a security-hardening outbound rule that permitted only UDP 3478–3481, blocking the dynamic RTP port range required for Teams media when Media Bypass is used. This change produced no audio for external calls; temporarily opening the wider UDP/RTP range restored audio immediately, confirming the firewall rule as the root cause [Data: Entities (303,442,443); Relationships (739,741,742,733)]. Firewall logs and change records tied the timing of the outbound restriction to the onset of audio issues, showing this was a local change management failure with direct impact to voice/media availability [Data: Relationships (738,739)]. ## Blueprint and operational docs exist but were not enforced by the post‑patch validation process. An authoritative Secure Web Publishing blueprint and versioned guidance (BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2 / WEB PUBLISHING BLUEPRINT) defines required behaviors: TLS termination at the WAF, required Portal-App-Template, and documented header rewrite rules and validation after patches. The team retrieved the blueprint from DMS to align remediation, indicating awareness of the standard; however, the blueprint's requirement to validate WAF profiles and preserve custom rewrite rules was not enforced automatically during the patch that reset profiles, producing configuration drift [Data: Entities (307,339,356); Relationships (508,512,562)]. This gap between blueprint and operational enforcement—where a profile reload caused a prohibited Default-Strict to be applied despite the blueprint forbidding it—exposes the environment to repeat regressions unless validation and change controls are hardened [Data: Relationships (548,497)]. ## Operational ownership, escalation path, and remediation were used correctly but could be formalized into runbooks and automated checks. The high-priority ticket TI-1594408 was owned initially by Employee 29 (helpdesk) who performed initial checks and escalated to BU Network & Security; BU Network & Security then took ownership and applied WAF/reverse proxy configuration changes to restore service, with monitoring confirming 24 hours stability before closure—showing correct functional escalation and remediation occurred [Data: Entities (295,298,299); Relationships (479,487,476)]. The artifacts show manual steps and handoffs (tests, template restores, monitoring) that resolved the incident, but the manual nature of these actions recommends formalizing a post‑patch WAF validation runbook, automated configuration drift detection, and explicit pre/post patch checklists to reduce mean‑time‑to‑repair and recurrence risk [Data: Relationships (479,513)].
